additionalTime: 48 to 72 hours dough fermentation plus 3 hours dough warm-up
author: Robert Carr
description: A large 15- to 16-inch pesto pizza built for a Baking Steel Pro, with a 72-hour cold-fermented crust, pistachio-basil pesto, whole-milk mozzarella, provolone, Pecorino Romano, thin mortadella, burrata or stracciatella, chopped pistachios, lemon-dressed arugula, and a light lemon-chile honey finish.
equipment: Baking Steel Pro or 16 x 16-inch baking steel
Covered dough container or large zip-top bag
Food processor, mini chopper, or mortar and pestle
Room-temperature rimless baking sheet, for finishing if the bottom crust browns early
ingredients: 285 grams high-protein bread flour, preferably Central Milling, King Arthur Sir Lancelot, or another strong bread flour
15 grams stone-ground whole wheat flour, or 15 grams more bread flour for the easiest stretch
195 grams cool water, about 3/4 cup plus 1 tablespoon plus 1/2 teaspoon
1/2 teaspoon honey for the dough
Scant 1/8 teaspoon instant yeast, about 0.4 grams
8 grams salt for the dough: about 1 1/2 level teaspoons fine sea salt, 1 3/4 level teaspoons Morton Coarse Kosher Salt, or 2 1/2 level teaspoons Diamond Crystal kosher salt
1 teaspoon extra-virgin olive oil for the dough, plus 1 teaspoon for the dough container
1 to 2 tablespoons semolina flour or fine cornmeal, for dusting the peel
Optional dough shortcut: 450 to 500 grams prepared dough from Baking Steel 72 Hour Pizza Dough Mix for one large 15- to 16-inch pizza
6 tablespoons roasted unsalted shelled pistachios, divided: 1/4 cup for pesto and 2 tablespoons chopped for finishing
2 packed cups fresh basil leaves for pesto, plus 8 to 10 small basil leaves for finishing
1 small garlic clove
1 1/2 ounces Pecorino Romano, finely grated, divided
1 lemon, zested and juiced, divided
1/4 cup plus 4 teaspoons extra-virgin olive oil, divided
1/4 teaspoon fine sea salt or Morton Coarse Kosher Salt for the pesto, plus 1/8 teaspoon for the arugula and small pinches as needed
1/8 teaspoon black pepper for the pesto, plus 1/8 teaspoon for the arugula
1/4 teaspoon anchovy paste for the pesto, optional but recommended
6 ounces low-moisture whole-milk mozzarella from a block, freshly shredded
1 1/2 ounces provolone piccante or sharp deli provolone, finely diced or shredded
3 ounces burrata or stracciatella for a 15-inch pizza, or 4 ounces for a true 16-inch pizza, drained on paper towels for 10 minutes
3 ounces very thin mortadella for a 15-inch pizza, or 4 ounces for a true 16-inch pizza, torn into loose 2-inch ribbons
1 1/2 cups baby arugula for a 15-inch pizza, or 2 cups for a true 16-inch pizza
1 tablespoon honey for the lemon-chile honey
1/4 teaspoon Calabrian chile paste for the lemon-chile honey, optional, or 1/8 teaspoon red pepper flakes
Small pinch flaky sea salt for finishing, optional
instructions: Using Baking Steel 72 Hour Pizza Dough Mix? Skip to step 13. Using ready-made dough? Skip to step 15 and use the prepared dough amount listed in the ingredients.

Two to three days before baking, make the scratch dough by whisking 195 grams cool water, 1/2 teaspoon honey, and 8 grams salt in a large mixing bowl until the honey dissolves and the salt is mostly dissolved.
Whisk the scant 1/8 teaspoon instant yeast, about 0.4 grams, into the salted honey water.
Add 285 grams high-protein bread flour and 15 grams whole wheat flour to the bowl with the yeasted water.
Mix with a dough scraper, spatula, or your hand for 1 to 2 minutes, until no dry flour remains and the dough looks shaggy.
Cover the bowl and rest the dough for 20 minutes so the flour hydrates before kneading.
Drizzle 1 teaspoon extra-virgin olive oil over the dough.
Knead or fold the dough in the bowl for 3 to 4 minutes, until the oil is absorbed and the dough looks smoother but still slightly tacky.
Cover the bowl again and rest the dough at room temperature for 30 minutes.
With wet hands, stretch one side of the dough up and fold it over the center, then rotate the bowl and repeat 3 more times so all 4 sides have been folded.
Shape the dough into a tight ball, lightly coat a covered dough container or large zip-top bag with 1 teaspoon extra-virgin olive oil, and place the dough inside.
Cover the dough and refrigerate it for 48 to 72 hours.

If using Baking Steel 72 Hour Pizza Dough Mix instead of the scratch dough, pour the mix into a bowl, add 1 3/4 cups water, knead for 2 to 3 minutes, rest the covered dough at room temperature for 12 to 24 hours, then refrigerate the covered dough for 2 days.

For the Baking Steel mix, portion 450 grams prepared dough for a 15-inch pizza or 500 grams prepared dough for a 16-inch pizza with a thicker rim about 3 hours before baking, shape that portion into a smooth ball, and save the remaining dough for another pizza or bread.
About 3 hours before baking, remove the cold dough from the refrigerator.
If the dough has spread in the container, gently tighten it back into a smooth ball without heavily kneading or degassing it.

Place the dough ball in a lightly oiled bowl or container, cover it, and let it warm at room temperature for about 3 hours, until relaxed, slightly puffy, and no longer refrigerator-cold in the center.
About 75 minutes before baking, place the Baking Steel Pro on the second-highest rack, ideally about 5 to 6 inches below the broiler element.
Preheat the oven to 550 degrees F for 60 to 75 minutes so the Baking Steel Pro is fully saturated with heat before the pizza goes in.

While the steel preheats, toast all 6 tablespoons roasted pistachios in a small dry skillet over medium-low heat for 2 to 3 minutes, shaking the skillet often, until the nuts smell fragrant but do not darken.
Transfer the toasted pistachios to a cutting board, chop 2 tablespoons for finishing, and leave 1/4 cup whole or roughly chopped for the pesto.

Make the pesto by adding the 1/4 cup pistachios, 2 packed cups fresh basil leaves, 1 small garlic clove, 1 ounce finely grated Pecorino Romano, 1/2 teaspoon finely grated lemon zest, 1/4 teaspoon fine sea salt or Morton Coarse Kosher Salt, 1/8 teaspoon black pepper, and 1/4 teaspoon anchovy paste if using to a food processor or mini chopper.
Pulse the pesto mixture 8 to 10 times, scraping the bowl once, until the basil and pistachios are finely chopped but not pureed smooth.
Add 1/4 cup extra-virgin olive oil to the pesto and pulse 3 to 5 times more, just until spoonable.
Stir 1 teaspoon fresh lemon juice into the pesto, then taste it; it should be bold, salty, nutty, and bright because only a thin measured layer goes on the pizza.
Measure 1/4 cup pesto for a 15-inch pizza or 1/3 cup pesto for a true 16-inch pizza, then reserve 1 tablespoon pesto for the finished rim.
Freshly shred 6 ounces low-moisture whole-milk mozzarella from a block.
Finely dice or shred 1 1/2 ounces provolone piccante or sharp deli provolone.
Toss together the 6 ounces shredded mozzarella, 1 1/2 ounces provolone, and 1/4 ounce finely grated Pecorino Romano so the cheese blend distributes evenly.
Keep the cheese blend cold until assembly so the cheese melts cleanly instead of oiling out on the peel.
Drain 3 ounces burrata or stracciatella for a 15-inch pizza, or 4 ounces for a true 16-inch pizza, on paper towels or in a fine-mesh strainer for 10 minutes so it tastes creamy instead of watery.
Tear the very thin mortadella into loose 2-inch ribbons: use 3 ounces for a 15-inch pizza or 4 ounces for a true 16-inch pizza.

Make the lemon-chile honey by warming 1 tablespoon honey, 1/4 teaspoon Calabrian chile paste or 1/8 teaspoon red pepper flakes if using, 1/2 teaspoon fresh lemon juice, and a tiny pinch of fine sea salt or Morton Coarse Kosher Salt in a small saucepan or microwave-safe bowl just until loose and glossy.

Make the arugula finish by tossing 1 1/2 cups baby arugula for a 15-inch pizza or 2 cups for a true 16-inch pizza with 1 teaspoon extra-virgin olive oil, 1 teaspoon fresh lemon juice, 1/8 teaspoon fine sea salt or Morton Coarse Kosher Salt, and 1/8 teaspoon black pepper.
Stir the reserved 1 tablespoon pesto with 1 teaspoon extra-virgin olive oil to make a loose post-bake rim oil.
Dust a 16-inch pizza peel with 1 tablespoon semolina flour or fine cornmeal.
Lightly flour the counter, place the room-temperature dough on the counter, and press from the center outward with your fingertips, leaving the outer 3/4 inch untouched and puffy.
Stretch the dough into a 15- to 16-inch round, keeping the center thin and the rim slightly thicker.
If the dough springs back while stretching, cover it and rest it for 5 minutes, then continue stretching.
Transfer the stretched dough to the semolina-dusted peel and gently shake the peel once to confirm the dough slides.
If any part sticks to the peel, lift that edge and dust a little more semolina flour or fine cornmeal underneath before topping.
Spread the measured 1/4 cup pesto for a 15-inch pizza or 1/3 cup pesto for a true 16-inch pizza over the dough in a very thin, even layer, leaving a 3/4-inch rim uncovered.
Scatter about three-fourths of the cold mozzarella-provolone-Pecorino blend evenly over the pesto.

Scatter the remaining mozzarella-provolone-Pecorino blend over any spots where the pesto is fully exposed; a little green showing through is good, but heavy exposed pesto can scorch under the broiler.
Gently shake the peel again before launching the topped pizza; the pizza should slide freely as one piece.
If any spot sticks after topping, lift that edge with floured fingers or a bench scraper and dust a small pinch of semolina flour or fine cornmeal underneath.
Launch the pizza onto the fully preheated Baking Steel Pro as soon as it slides freely, ideally within 2 minutes of adding pesto, so moisture and oil from the pesto do not glue the dough to the peel.
Bake the pizza at 550 degrees F for 2 1/2 minutes with the broiler off.
At 2 1/2 minutes, rotate the pizza 180 degrees with the peel so the back edge does not brown faster than the front edge.
Check the bottom crust by lifting one edge with the peel; at 2 1/2 minutes it should be set and lightly browned, not floppy, dark brown, or black.
If the bottom is still pale and flexible at 2 1/2 minutes, leave the pizza directly on the steel for 30 to 60 seconds more before using the broiler.
If the bottom already has dark brown spots at 2 1/2 minutes, slide the pizza onto a room-temperature rimless baking sheet before continuing so the bottom stops darkening on the steel.
Turn the broiler to high and broil for 30 to 75 seconds, watching constantly, until the rim blisters and the cheese bubbles with scattered golden spots.
If the rim darkens before the cheese is fully bubbling, turn the broiler off and finish at 550 degrees F for 30 to 90 seconds.

If the top still needs more color after 75 seconds under the broiler, turn the broiler off, return the oven to 550 degrees F, and bake for 1 to 2 minutes more while checking the bottom every 30 seconds.
If the bottom becomes deep golden before the top is finished, move the pizza onto the room-temperature rimless baking sheet and finish the top under the broiler for 30 to 60 seconds.
Transfer the baked pizza to a cutting board and let it rest for 2 minutes so the cheese settles and the crust stays crisp when sliced.
Brush only the exposed outer rim with 2 to 3 teaspoons of the reserved pesto rim oil while the crust is still hot; avoid brushing the center so the pizza does not turn greasy.
Drape the torn mortadella over the hot pizza in loose folds: use 3 ounces for a 15-inch pizza or 4 ounces for a true 16-inch pizza.
Spoon or tear the drained burrata or stracciatella over the mortadella and cheese: use 3 ounces for a 15-inch pizza or 4 ounces for a true 16-inch pizza.
Sprinkle the chopped 2 tablespoons toasted pistachios and the remaining 1/4 ounce finely grated Pecorino Romano over the pizza.
Tear 8 to 10 small fresh basil leaves over the pizza and grate a light pinch of fresh lemon zest over the burrata.
Pile the dressed arugula lightly over the center and around the mortadella, leaving some rim exposed.
Drizzle 1 to 2 teaspoons lemon-chile honey over the mortadella, burrata, and arugula; use the lighter amount if you want the crust to stay crisper.
Add a small pinch of flaky sea salt if the burrata or rim tastes underseasoned.
Slice the pizza and serve immediately while the bottom crust is crisp, the rim is tender and blistered, the mortadella is warm, and the burrata is still creamy and cool.
notes: This pizza uses pistachio-basil pesto, mortadella, burrata, lemon, arugula, and toasted nuts instead of a red sauce or white cheese base.

For more crust flavor and control, use the scratch dough and cold-ferment it for the full 72 hours. The small amount of whole wheat flour adds nutty flavor without making the large dough hard to stretch.

The scratch dough makes about one 500-gram dough ball, enough for a large 15- to 16-inch pizza with a crisp bottom, a tender rim, and enough structure for pesto, cheese, mortadella, burrata, and arugula.
If using a mix, Baking Steel 72 Hour Pizza Dough Mix is a good shortcut for this recipe because it is designed for a 72-hour cold ferment and uses Central Milling organic flour, sea salt, and yeast.
One prepared Baking Steel dough mix pack can make 3 larger pizzas if divided evenly. Use 450 grams prepared dough for a 15-inch pizza or 500 grams prepared dough for a true 16-inch pizza.

If buying ready-made dough instead, ask a local pizzeria, Italian market, or bakery for 450 to 500 grams of cold-fermented dough made for a hot deck oven. Avoid canned dough, par-baked crusts, dough that smells sweet, dough that feels oily, or dough that tears immediately when stretched.
For home ovens at 550 degrees F, strong bread flour usually bakes better than pure Neapolitan 00 flour because it browns and crisps more reliably on a steel.
For the first bake, stretch to 15 or 15 1/2 inches instead of forcing the full 16 inches. The Baking Steel Pro is 16 x 16 inches, so a true 16-inch round leaves almost no launch margin.
Use a 16-inch peel for this pizza. A smaller peel makes a 15- to 16-inch dough difficult to launch cleanly.
Do not use parchment paper at 550 degrees F or under the broiler. A semolina-dusted peel gives a clean launch and avoids scorched paper edges.
Preheating the Baking Steel Pro for 60 to 75 minutes matters because the 3/8-inch steel needs time to heat through. A short preheat can leave the top cooked before the bottom crisps.
This pesto pizza has less tomato moisture than a red-sauce pizza and more exposed oil, so the bottom can brown fast. Start checking the bottom at 2 1/2 minutes.
Use the room-temperature rimless baking sheet if the bottom browns faster than the top. It lets the top finish without more direct steel contact.
The broiler is only for finishing. Watch constantly once the broiler is on because the rim, cheese, and pesto can go from blistered to burned quickly.
Keep the pesto measured and thin. Too much oil can soften the center crust and make the pizza stick to the peel.
Cover most of the pesto with cheese before baking. Protected pesto stays green and aromatic; large uncovered streaks can scorch under the broiler.
Use roasted unsalted pistachios and toast them briefly even if they are already roasted. The short toast refreshes the nut flavor without making the finish taste bitter.
The anchovy paste is optional but useful. At 1/4 teaspoon it deepens the pesto and cheese without making the pizza taste fishy.

Use low-moisture whole-milk mozzarella from a block because it melts well without flooding the pizza. Avoid pre-shredded mozzarella if possible because anti-caking starches can make the melt drier and less glossy.

Burrata or stracciatella goes on after baking so it stays creamy and does not waterlog the fast steel bake. Drain it for 10 minutes and use 3 ounces on a 15-inch pizza or 4 ounces only for a true 16-inch pizza.
Mortadella goes on after baking so it warms gently instead of drying out, curling hard, or turning greasy under the broiler.
Dress the arugula lightly and add it after baking. The lemon and greens cut the richness from the mortadella, burrata, pistachios, and cheese.
The lemon-chile honey is a finishing accent, not a sauce. Use 1 to 2 teaspoons so the pizza gets a few bright, sweet, spicy bites without making the crust sticky.
Brush the rim with pesto oil after baking, not before baking. Post-bake pesto oil seasons the crust without encouraging the rim to burn.
If the dough resists stretching, rest it for 5 minutes. Forcing tight dough knocks out gas and can make the rim dense.
If the bottom is too dark before the top is browned, use the room-temperature sheet sooner during that bake; next time, either move the steel one rack lower or start checking the bottom at 2 minutes.
If the top browns before the bottom crisps, keep the broiler off longer; next time, preheat the steel for the full 75 minutes and bake 1 rack position lower before using the broiler.
If the center is soft, use 1 tablespoon less pesto next time, drain the burrata longer, use the 15-inch topping amounts, and stretch the center thinner before topping.
Leftover pesto keeps covered in the refrigerator for 2 days. Press plastic wrap directly onto the surface or cover it with a thin film of olive oil to slow browning.
title: Mortadella Pistachio Pesto Pizza
totalTime: 1 hour 10 minutes active, plus 48 to 72 hours dough fermentation and 3 hours dough warm-up
